item.title: HSGAC Hearing Recap: Ransomware Attacks and Ransom Payments Enabled by Cryptocurrency
item.description: During a recent HSGAC hearing, Coveware had the opportunity to provide testimony on how ransomware attacks are impacting US companies, and the importance mandatory reporting.

item.title: Fewer Ransomware Victims Pay, as Median Ransom Falls in Q2 2022
item.description: Ransomware actors became more fluid in Q2 2022 as attribution becomes harder, and fewer victims succumb to paying cyber criminals.

item.title: Uber Verdict Raises New Risks for Ransom Payments
item.description: The conviction of a high profile security executive who paid to suppress a data leak has created a new dimension of risk for security executives.

item.title: Improved Security and Backups Result in Record Low Number of Ransomware Payments
item.description: Only 37% of ransomware victims paid a ransom in Q4, a record low as security and backup continuity investments pay off.

item.title: Big Game Hunting is back despite decreasing Ransom Payment Amounts
item.description: Ransomware threat actors are moving back up-market in search of lost profits as the cyber extortion economy seeks to halt its contraction.

item.title: Ransom Monetization Rates Fall to Record Low Despite Jump In Average Ransom Payments
item.description: As ransomware affiliates are paid less frequently, they have adapted their strategies to compensate for the shifting dynamics of cyber extortion.

item.title: Scattered Ransomware Attribution Blurs Focus on IR Fundamentals
item.description: In Q3 2023 a series of ransomware attacks by similar threat actors created headlines and blurred the lines of attribution.

item.title: New Ransomware Reporting Requirements Kick in as Victims Increasingly Avoid Paying
item.description: A lower percentage of ransomware victims are paying, as new regulations begin to elicit more and more public disclosure of ransomware incidents.

item.title: RaaS devs hurt their credibility by cheating affiliates in Q1 2024
item.description: RaaS developers were caught cheating their affiliates, shaking the trust in the RaaS model following several high profile law enforcement actions.

item.title: Ransomware actors pivot away from major brands in Q2 2024
item.description: Unaffiliated ‘lone wolf’ threat actors carry out a greater share of attacks as they attempt to obfuscate their identity in Q2 2024.

item.title: Law enforcement doxxing raises risk profile for threat actors
item.description: In Q3 2024 Law enforcement actions disrupted infrastructure and publicized the identity of several prolific ransomware threat actors

item.title: Misleading Metrics: Unraveling Ransom Payment Statistics in Australia
item.description: A recent Australian Financial Review (AFR) article contained statistics about ransomware payment trends that require some deeper inspection.

item.title: Will Law Enforcement success against ransomware continue in 2025?
item.description: 2024 was a banner year for Law enforcement agency actions against ransomware and cybercrime groups. Will the new administration ensure this momentum continues?

item.title: The organizational structure of ransomware threat actor groups is evolving before our eyes
item.description: The Ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) model has not recovered from law enforcement disruption, and the entrance of novice actors along with non-Russian state-linked cybercriminals has led to uncertain outcomes for victims.

item.title: Targeted social engineering is en vogue as ransom payment sizes increase
item.description: Several ransomware groups used highly targeted social engineering tactics to create a major impact across several industry sectors in Q2 2025.

item.title: Insider Threats Loom while Ransom Payment Rates Plummet
item.description: The percentage of companies choosing to pay ransoms dropped significantly, while threat actors shift their tactics in response to decreasing profits.

item.title: Obscura Ransomware: A Case Study in Ransomware Data Loss
item.description: Discover how Obscura ransomware corrupts encrypted files beyond recovery, and why technical validation is key to smart ransom response decisions

item.title: Nitrogen Ransomware: ESXi malware has a bug!
item.description: Nitrogen ransomware was derived from the previously leaked Conti 2 builder code, and is similar to Nitrogen ransomware, but a coding mistake in the ESXi malware causes it to encrypt all the files with the wrong public key, irrevocably corrupting them. This means that even the threat actor is incapable of decrypting them, and that victims that are without viable backups have no ability to recover their ESXi encrypted servers. Paying a ransom will not assist these victims, as the decryption key/ tool will not work.

item.title: Why Zero-Day Downstream Mass Data Extortion Campaigns are Losing Their Bite
item.description: Are we seeing the extinction of mass data exfiltration campaigns? The stats demonstrate these attacks are losing their efficacy.

item.title: Patch management goes from hard, to ludicrous in the agentic AI era
item.description: The release of agentic AI is compressing the nature of patch management and how defenders must prepare for the future of cyber attacks. This is increasing pressure on patch velocity, compensating controls, and dependency visibility.
